steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content:
Clause 1 (main clause of the first sentence): "the 2021 exhibition The New Woman Behind the Camera set out to provide a wide-ranging overview of photography by women in the 1920s through the [1950s]." The opening "Featuring works by...Neulander-Simon" is a participial modifier attached to this clause.

Clause 2: "its efforts were largely successful," preceded by the introductory participial phrase "given the collection's breadth of more than 120 photos." Both sides of the blank are full independent clauses.

steps.title: Apply the comma + FANBOYS rule
steps.content:
Two independent clauses must be joined by a comma plus a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so) — or separated by a semicolon or period. A comma alone produces a comma splice; no punctuation produces a run-on.
  • A. "1950s, and" — correct: comma + coordinating conjunction properly joins the two independent clauses.
  • B. "1950s and" — incorrect: missing the required comma before "and" joining two independent clauses.
  • C. "1950s" — incorrect: no punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on.
  • D. "1950s," — incorrect: a comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice.
The answer is A.
